steps.text: Maintain the integrity of target microorganism(s) until samples are analyzed.
Include a viability assay within a detection method if the study's purpose is to determine human health risk.
Hold or incubate samples under specific conditions to allow cells to resuscitate if certain bacteria strains are in a viable but nonculturable state.
Collect and store samples using techniques that differ depending on whether viable or nonviable organisms are detected.
Conduct sampling and downstream processing at lower temperatures (4°C) within hours to days for viable organisms.
Specify conditions such as incubation, growth media or anaerobic conditions in sampling technique SOPs based on project goals.
Document data parameters or sample-matrix parameters during sampling as required or suggested.
Consider the impact of sample collection and transportation on parameter of interest when sending split or replicate samples to a laboratory.
Collect various sampling site and matrix parameters as examples include date, location, time, weather conditions, and more.
Use blanks and duplicate, replicate or spiked samples for QC.
Run an unspiked matrix blank to measure background levels.
Run unspiked blank samples each time a sampling procedure is completed.
Acquire duplicate or replicate samples whenever feasible.
Run spiked samples or positive controls for each sampling procedure.
Run trip or travel blanks to document contamination during shipping and handling.
Document all relevant sample information during sampling.
Perform field measurements like pH and temperature on a separate subsample.
Perform regular quality compliance checks.
Document reviews and audits formally.
Check chemical preservatives for efficacy.
Ensure timely transport to minimize sample deterioration.
Evaluate sample integrity elements.
Verify sample integrity with appropriate QC steps.
Protect samples from contamination and deterioration before their arrival to a laboratory.
Ensure equipment is clean—sterile whenever possible—and in good working condition.
Use sterile sample containers and keep them in a clean environment and away from dust, dirt and fumes.
Do not touch or handle the inner portion of the sample container.
Clean and sterilize reusable containers and prove they are free of quantifiable target analyte before use.
Use sterile gloves and other necessary techniques, such as washing hands and wearing facemasks, to reduce sample contamination.
Place samples in sealed containers after collection to prevent contamination during storage and/or transport.
Consider storage conditions, holding time, and transport procedures.
Record when samples arrive to document them as they pass through the laboratory’s analytical systems.
Log in and store samples to minimize deterioration, and record sample conditions and storage locations.
Record subsampling, sample splitting, or sample pretreatment to increase stability.
Clearly and uniquely mark the source and identity of samples to avoid confusion.
Make arrangements for disposal or appropriate disposition of samples and sampling materials when no longer needed.
Follow BMBL for biosafety.
Maintain an accurate written record to create a chain of custody for the sample from collection to disposal.
Document who has possessed the sample(s) and all analyses performed on the samples.
Follow chain-of-custody procedures when handling samples and data to ensure no tampering occurs.
Keep a minimum number of people to collect and handle samples and data.
Allow only people associated with the project to handle samples and data.
Document the transfer of samples and data on chain-of-custody forms and maintain site security for these samples.
Ensure chain-of-custody forms accompany samples and data.
Ensure samples and data include identification that is legible and written with permanent ink.
Follow the chain-of-custody steps during sampling preparations, taking the sample, transporting, receipt, storage, transfer, analysis, and data record keeping.

constraints.text: Samples must be protected from contamination and deterioration.
BMBL should be followed for biosafety.
Samples and wastes may be subject to DOT hazardous material regulations.
Air shipments must comply with IATA Dangerous Goods Regulations.
All hazardous goods shipments must comply with regulations and guidance.
Personnel with approved DOT/IATA training must perform all shipments of potentially hazardous materials.
Shipments should be packaged, labeled, and shipped according to appropriate regulations.
Samples or wastes should be stored in a secure area protected from vermin and adverse weather conditions.
Hazardous and nonhazardous samples and wastes must be separated.

steps.text: Prepare a suitable report for placing in the public docket.
Address the sampling technique validation topics outlined in the guidance document.
Provide background information on sampling technique development.
Describe the sampling technique.
Describe the sampling technique validation practices.
Document changes made to the sampling technique as a result of the validation studies.
Provide recommendations for future work.
Provide an overall summary of the validation report.
Describe how selectivity was evaluated and how the sampling plan identified and addressed interferences.
Describe the sensitivity of the technique and how the sampling plan addressed it.
Describe how specificity was evaluated and how the sampling plan addressed it.

Describe how the sampling plan addresses viability assays, if required/applicable, and what the requirements are for maintaining/preserving viability. Describe sample security from the collection site to the analytical laboratory.
Discuss the number of items or the quantity that constitutes an adequate sample for the technique, and whether the samples are composites or grab.
Describe how the sampling technique matches the performance of the applicable analytical assay technique(s).
Describe sampling technique performance after experiencing minor changes in operating or environmental conditions.
Describe the applicable resource requirements, such as personnel, skills, and equipment, as well as the required measurements to be taken, such as site parameters and matrix parameters.
Describe safety concerns and procedures that should be addressed, including personal protective equipment and first aid as well as physical and chemical hazards.
Describe the QC/QA checks used.
Discuss technique development.
Discuss validation testing results. Evaluate the testing, including comparison with reference materials and preparations, acceptance criteria and recommendations.
Discuss sampling technique changes that were made as a result of the validation studies.
Discuss formal acceptance/rejection of work.
Provide recommendations for future work.
